sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after DOWN
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== Colored squares ===")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ('R', 'b', 'N', 'M'):
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if min(rows) < 50:  # Main play area only
            print(f"Color={node['color']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check gray strips
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Gray strips ===")
for r in range(10, 45):
    row_str = lines[r][10:17]
    if 'gg' in row_str:
        print(f"  Row {r}: '{row_str}'")

# Check trail
print("\n=== Trail row ===")
for r in [32, 33]:
    print(f"  Row {r}: {lines[r][10:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Now I see something interesting. After moving DOWN:
- Player at rows 30-35
- Red at rows 13-16 (didn't move!)

So the squares DON'T all move together. Only the square that the trail touches (Green) moves with the player. The other squares (Red and Blue) stay in place.
This changes everything! The mechanic is:
- The trail touches one square at a time (the one at the same vertical position as the player)
- When the player moves UP/DOWN, only the touched square moves with the player
- The other squares stay in place
- LEFT/RIGHT extend/retract the trail, which also moves the touched square horizontally
So the goal might be to:

2. Use LEFT/RIGHT to position that square

4. Repeat until all squares are in the correct positions
